1:
The chapter opens with a foreign king's friendship, and the witnesses read Hiram's embassy as both a piece of ancient history and a lesson in mutual dependence. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the first moral from the unequal partnership: there is no man of such sufficiency in himself that he has no need of his neighbours, nor any so great that he has not reason to be thankful for their help. David ruled a large kingdom and Hiram a very little one, yet David could not build himself a house to his mind until Hiram furnished him with both workmen and materials; and this, Henry says, is a reason why we should despise none, but, as we have opportunity, be obliging to all.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke keeps to the plain sequence of events: Hiram king of Tyre sends artificers and materials to David to build him a house, and the whole transaction is related more fully in the parallel account, which Clarke simply cross-references at 2 Samuel 5:11. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fills in the philology and the architecture. He notes that the Hebrew text of Chronicles spells the name Hiram, though its own margin writes Huram, while the Septuagint and all the ancient versions of both books agree on Hiram; the messengers are ambassadors; and the timber of cedars was felled from Lebanon and borne by sea to the coast for the building.

Ellicott lingers over the word rendered 'house.' It means a palace, he explains, the same term by which the Temple itself would later be called 'the house' as well as 'the palace'; and he sets David's building in its wider setting, recalling the numerous records of palace-building left by the Assyrian and Babylonian sovereigns, for whom the cedar of Lebanon was likewise a favourite material. The masons and carpenters are, more literally, craftsmen of walls and craftsmen of timber, so that Samuel's fuller phrase is here compressed. Together the witnesses turn a single verse of diplomacy into a portrait of the young kingdom taking its place among the nations, supplied by a neighbour's goodwill and housed in cedar like the great kings of the East, whose very favour toward David — as the next verse will insist — was itself a token of the favour of God.
2:
David's inward recognition of God's hand is where the witnesses fix their attention, and they draw doctrine out of a single verb. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the opening 'David knew that Jehovah had appointed him king,' and reads the willing alliance of the powerful sovereign of Tyre as the very thing David so understood — for the favour of man is sometimes a sign of the approval of God, always when it results from well-doing, as it did with Joseph in Genesis 39:21 and with the boy Jesus who grew in favour with God and man in Luke 2:52. This verse, Ellicott adds, helps us to understand how David could be called a man after God's own heart: his innate humility recognises at once that the ground of his exaltation was not personal but national.

His fellow grammatical-historical witnesses Keil and Delitzsch work at the grammar behind that thought. Where the parallel at 2 Samuel 5:12 says that the LORD 'had lifted up' David's kingdom, the Chronicler writes rather that 'his kingdom had been lifted up on high,' using an unusual passive form; and the little phrase 'on high' is, they note, a favourite intensive of the Chronicler, recurring again and again through his book. Ellicott agrees that the Chronicler's word for kingdom is the more modern of the two terms, later than that used in the older narrative.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns from the grammar to the heart of a settled king. It is a great satisfaction to a wise man to be settled, he says, and to a good man to see the special providences of God in his settlement; David could not be easy nor think himself happy until he perceived that the LORD had confirmed him king over Israel — 'Who shall unfix me if God hath fixed me?' And Henry presses the reason the verse itself gives for the exaltation: David's kingdom was lifted up on high not for his own sake, that he might look great, but because of God's people Israel, that he might be their guide and protector. We must look on all our advancements as designed for our usefulness; we are blessed in order that we may be blessings, as Abraham was told in Genesis 12:2, for we are not born, nor do we live, for ourselves. The witnesses do not divide; they gather around a king whose first act of power is to confess that the power is God's and the purpose his people's.
3:
From the settled throne the narrative turns to the growing household, and the witnesses weigh David's marriages with a candour that neither excuses nor exaggerates. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply carry the record forward: David took more wives at Jerusalem and begat more sons and daughters, whose names the following verses will list. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes that the Chronicler has abbreviated his source, which in the parallel at 2 Samuel 5:13 reads 'concubines and wives from Jerusalem, after he had come from Hebron.' The concubines are dropped here, Ellicott is careful to say, not out of any wish to spare David's memory, for they are openly mentioned elsewhere in the Chronicler's own genealogy at 1 Chronicles 3:9.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry alone presses the moral tension the verse holds. It was David's infirmity, he says plainly, that when he settled in his kingdom he took more wives — a multiplying of marriages into which prosperity easily betrays, and against the danger of which the law of the king had warned. Yet Henry will not let the failing swallow the mercy that accompanies it: the numerous issue with which David was blessed added to his honour and his strength, for 'children are a heritage of the LORD,' the very word of Psalms 127:3. So the same verse records at once a weakness and a blessing, the king's fault and God's gift bound together.

Henry notes too that we have already had an account of David's children, not only in the older history but in this very book, at the Chronicler's genealogy in 1 Chronicles 3:1, and now here again — a repetition he takes as no accident, for it was the children's honour to have such a father. The witnesses thus hold the verse in a steady double light. They do not gloss over the multiplied wives as though prosperity carried no danger to the soul; nor do they read the crowded nursery as mere indulgence, since the sons and daughters are themselves a heritage from the LORD. It is the portrait of a man greatly favoured and not yet wholly mastered, whose house grows even as his throne is confirmed, and whose very family will be numbered, in the pages that follow, among the evidences of the divine prosperity resting upon him.
4:
The catalogue of David's Jerusalem-born sons opens here, and the two witnesses who pause over it are drawn at once to the textual variety in the lists and to one name of surpassing weight. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke counts the versions against each other: in the Hebrew of the parallel at 2 Samuel 5:14 only eleven persons are named, the Septuagint there has twenty-four, and here in Chronicles thirteen are given, all the ancient versions agreeing in that number though with certain varieties in the several names. The differences, he implies, are the ordinary wear of transmission, not a wound to the substance of the record.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the wording and to the genealogy behind it. 'His children,' he notes, is literally 'the born,' a different word from the one the older text uses; the list is repeated here because it stood at just this point in the document the historian was copying, and perhaps also as an instance of David's prosperity, which is the very topic of the section. He restores the conjunction that runs throughout the names — it should read 'and Nathan,' as the parallel has it — and then rests on that name, for it is from this son of David, Nathan, that Joseph, the husband of Mary of whom was born Jesus, traced his descent in the genealogy of Luke 3:23. The fuller list, Ellicott adds, may be compared with the Chronicler's own genealogy at 1 Chronicles 3:5.

So the witnesses give the verse two dimensions at once. On the surface it is a dry register of royal offspring, and its very dryness invites the text-critical eye that Clarke turns on the differing numbers. But beneath the register runs the thread that Ellicott follows: among these sons born in the newly won city stands Nathan, and among them stands Solomon, and through the one and the other the line will run down to Bethlehem and to the Son of David in whom the whole story finds its end. The names that seem only to swell the count of a prosperous king's household are, on the witnesses' reading, the quiet channel of the promise, and the section that means to display David's greatness turns out to be tracing, all unawares, the ancestry of a greater King.
7:
With Elishama, Beeliada, and Eliphalet the roll of David's Jerusalem-born sons is closed, and the witnesses treat the whole catalogue as a single unit rather than parsing its final names one by one. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, in setting out the list, carry it through to these last three and refer the reader, for the sons of David, to the fuller genealogy at 1 Chronicles 3:5 and following, where the same names recur with the small differences of spelling that mark such registers. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke had already prepared the reader for exactly those differences, observing that when the parallel at 2 Samuel 5:16 and the ancient versions are compared, the number of the sons varies and there are certain varieties in the names themselves — a caution that falls with special force on this closing verse, whose Beeliada appears elsewhere under another form.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gives the reason the whole list is set down here at all. It is repeated, he says, because it stood at this very point in the document the historian was copying, and perhaps also as an instance of David's prosperity, which is the topic of the section; the swelling tally of sons is itself a token of the divine favour resting on the king. So the last names of the catalogue are not idle. They complete the evidence that the LORD had lifted David's kingdom on high, crowding his house with heirs even as his throne was confirmed and his palace built.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry supplies the note of thanksgiving that the bare list withholds. These sons, like all the rest, are to be reckoned a heritage of the LORD, as the psalmist sings in Psalms 127:3; and it was their honour, Henry says, to have such a father. The witnesses thus refuse to let the closing names sink into mere genealogy. Taken together — Keil's careful listing, Clarke's textual candour, Ellicott's sense of the section's purpose, and Henry's gratitude — they read even this tail of the register as one more line in the record of a prospered king: a household granted, multiplied, and honoured by the God who had set David over Israel, and who was even now, through these very sons, threading the line of promise toward the Son of David yet to come.
8:
No sooner is the kingdom confirmed and the house built than the Philistines march, and the witnesses read their timing as the world's standing answer to a rising cause. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, opening his account of a triumph he takes to be the same as the older record, draws the warning first: let the attack the Philistines made upon David forbid us to be secure in any settlement or advancement, and teach us to expect molestation in this world; when we are most at ease, something may yet arise to vex us. Christ's kingdom, he adds, will be insulted by the serpent's seed, and especially when it makes any advance — the enemy stirs precisely because David has just been raised so high.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke marks the Philistines' hostile purpose in going up 'to seek David,' referring the reader to the fuller narrative at 2 Samuel 5:17. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens both the wording and the strategy. 'David was anointed,' he notes, where the older text says 'they had anointed David'; the verb is the one from which comes the very name Messiah. The Chronicler's added 'over all Israel' — the word 'all' being absent from the parallel — deliberately contrasts David's second election with his first, when he had been made king of Judah only. 'To seek David' is meant with hostile intent, the same use of the verb as in 1 Samuel 26:2; and 'went out against them' is, more literally, 'went out before them.'

Where the Chronicler has David go out to meet the enemy, Ellicott observes, the older narrative says he 'went down to the stronghold' — the term used of the castle of Zion in 1 Chronicles 11:5, though here more probably David's old refuge, the rock and cave of Adullam. The Philistine host would likely take its accustomed route up through the valley toward Jerusalem, and David went down to face it. The witnesses do not divide; together they set the scene of the first battle with a double emphasis — on the malice of an enemy provoked by David's very exaltation, and on the anointing that made him at once the Lord's chosen and the Philistines' mark. That the assault should follow so hard upon the anointing is, for Henry, the whole point: the higher God lifts his servant, the surer the world's opposition.
9:
The narrative doubles back to describe the invasion it has just announced, and the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott is the one witness who lingers over the manoeuvre. The verse, he explains, should be read as a resumption — 'Now the Philistines had come' — the account going back to pick up the thread from the report of their setting out; the Chronicler tells first that they went up to seek David and then returns to relate how they came and deployed. The invaders, Ellicott observes, had approached by another road than the one they usually took, a fresh line of advance that would bring them into the broad and fertile valley of Rephaim, south-west of Jerusalem, the classic corridor by which armies threatened the city.

That valley, he notes, is the same ground named earlier in the Chronicler's own record at 1 Chronicles 11:15, where three of David's mighty men broke through the Philistine garrison to fetch him water from the well of Bethlehem — so that the reader who knows the book already associates this plain with Philistine encampment and with daring counter-stroke alike. Here the enemy 'spread themselves' across it, and Ellicott catches even the choice of word: the Chronicler has given an easier, gentler term than the harsher one used in the parallel history, a small softening of the verb that pictures the hostile army fanning out over the open valley floor.

Brief as the notice is, and carried by a single witness, it does its work in the chapter's design. The Philistines are not skirmishing at the border but pouring into the very approaches of the newly won capital, spreading their forces in the open valley as men who mean to overwhelm. The stage is thus fully set for the question the next verse will record, for David, faced with an enemy deployed in strength across the plain, does not rush to arms but turns first to inquire of God. The bare topography of the ninth verse, on Ellicott's close reading, is the dark and level ground against which that inquiry, and the victory it secures, will stand out.
10:
Before he draws a sword David asks a question, and the witnesses make his inquiry the moral centre of the whole campaign. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had set it down as the lesson of the chapter: let David's enquiry of God, once and again, direct us in all our ways to acknowledge him — in distress to fly to him, when we are wronged to appeal to him, and, when we know not what to do, to ask counsel at his oracles, putting ourselves under his direction and begging him to show us the right way. The king with an army at his back will not move until heaven has spoken.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke renders the act with the Targum's gloss, that 'David consulted the Word of the LORD.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the practical question of how such consulting was done. It was through the high priest, he explains, who sought divine direction by means of the Urim and Thummim, the sacred lots carried in a pouch upon the breast and fastened to the ephod — the appointed oracle whose institution is described in Exodus 28:30 and Numbers 27:21, and to which David had resorted before, calling for the ephod in his wilderness days at 1 Samuel 30:7. Ellicott also tidies the grammar: there should be a comma, not a question-mark, in the middle, for the whole sentence forms but a single question in the Hebrew, where the older text had given two distinct and disconnected questions, and the rest of the verse is here abridged.

The answer comes as plainly as the question: 'Go up; for I will deliver them into thine hand.' The witnesses do not divide; they agree that the significance lies less in the mechanics of the ephod than in the disposition it served. David, newly confirmed and freshly attacked, does not presume upon his anointing or his veteran skill in war, but submits the very question of whether to fight to the will of God, and receives both the commission and the promise from the same mouth. Henry's application stands over the scene: the man after God's own heart is known here not by his courage, which was never in doubt, but by his habit of acknowledging God in his ways and waiting for the word before he ventures the battle.
11:
The victory comes and David names the place for it, and the witnesses dwell on the metaphor of the bursting flood and the humility that reads it aright. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke gives the Targum's expansion of David's cry: 'The LORD hath broken the enemies of David like to the breaking of a potter's vessel full of water' — a shattering that scatters all at a stroke. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott recovers the geography and the theology together. 'They came up to Baal-perazim,' he notes — David and his troops, where the older text says simply 'David came'; the locality is now unknown, but its importance is vouched by the prophet Isaiah, who looked back to these very victories when he spoke of the LORD rising up 'as in mount Perazim' and being wroth 'as in the valley of Gibeon' to do his strange work, in Isaiah 28:21.

The name itself, Ellicott explains, means 'lord' or 'owner of breaches, of breakings-forth,' the plural of the word for a breach; David's forces perhaps charged down the slopes like a mountain torrent, sweeping all before them, so that the very gullies of the hillside lent the place its name. And true to his character, Ellicott says, David owns the mighty hand of God in the results of his own valour: where the king cries 'God hath broken in upon mine enemies by mine hand,' he confesses himself merely the instrument, in pointed contrast to the haughty self-confidence of the Assyrian conqueror whom Isaiah would later mock in Isaiah 10:15. David's own later prayer breathes the same spirit, Ellicott adds, comparing his confession at 1 Chronicles 17:16.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the devotional fruit. Let David's thankful acknowledgment of the hand of God in his successes, he urges, direct us to bring all our sacrifices of praise to God's altar, saying with the psalm, 'Not unto us, O LORD, not unto us, but to thy name give glory.' The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the wonder of the verse is not merely the rout of the Philistines but the theology folded into the name of the field: that the breach was God's breaking-forth and not the king's prowess, that the flood which swept the enemy away rose from heaven, and that David, standing victorious, had the grace to say so, and to fix the confession for ever in the name of the place.
12:
The routed Philistines leave their idols on the field, and the witnesses fasten on what David does with them and on an apparent clash between the two histories. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the discrepancy squarely. Where Chronicles says the Philistines 'left their gods there' and that, at David's command, 'they were burned with fire,' the older narrative says rather that 'David and his men carried them off,' as at 2 Samuel 5:21. The Chronicler's word 'gods,' Ellicott notes, is explanatory of the older 'images.' And the two statements, he insists, are not incompatible: both may well have stood in the same original text, the abandoned idols first seized as spoil and then, by the king's order, given to the flames.

What matters to the Chronicler, on Ellicott's reading, is the burning; for he is careful to record David's compliance with the law that commanded the graven images of the heathen to be destroyed by fire, the statute of Deuteronomy 7:25. Here is the sharp reversal that gives the verse its point: at an earlier and darker hour the Philistines had carried off the ark of God as a trophy; now their own gods are carried off and burned, and the idols that could not save their worshippers from David cannot save themselves from the fire.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the lesson the deed embodies. Let David's burning of the gods of the Philistines, when they fell into his hands, teach us a holy indignation against idolatry and all the remains of it — not a curious keeping of the enemy's images as spoil or ornament, but a resolute destroying of them as an abomination. The witnesses do not finally divide even over the seeming contradiction, since Ellicott dissolves it; and they close in agreement on the theology of the flames. David's first care after victory is not to enrich himself with the enemy's sacred plunder but to obey the law of his God and wipe out the idols, so that the same hand the LORD had used to break the Philistines is used to burn the gods they trusted — the whole episode a small, fierce parable that the idols of the nations are fuel, and the LORD alone is God.
13:
The Philistines, beaten once, will not accept the verdict, and the verse records their second muster in the same fatal valley. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes only, with his usual economy, that the Chronicler has slightly abridged his source here: where the parallel names the arena in full as the valley 'of Rephaim,' Chronicles says simply that the invaders 'yet again spread themselves abroad in the valley' — the same broad plain south-west of Jerusalem into which they had poured before. The word for their spreading is the one already used of the first invasion, so that the reader watches the whole hostile deployment repeated, stroke for stroke, upon the very ground of their earlier defeat.

That repetition is the heart of the verse. The enemy who was broken at Baal-perazim like a bursting flood regroups and returns, undeterred by the rout, to try the same approaches a second time. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, reading the whole narrative as a single lesson, finds here the confirmation of his warning that no settlement in this world is ever secure: Christ's kingdom will be insulted by the serpent's seed, and especially when it makes any advance, so that a first victory is no guarantee against a second assault. The Philistines' persistence is the persistence of all opposition to the cause of God, which is not overcome by one defeat and must be met again.

Yet the very sameness of the situation prepares a difference in the outcome, and the witnesses let the bare notice lean forward into what follows. David stands once more before an enemy spread across the valley of Rephaim, exactly as before; but the God who answers is not bound to repeat himself, and the next verse will show the LORD prescribing an entirely new tactic for the new battle. The thirteenth verse, brief and almost a mere echo of the ninth, thus serves the chapter's design: it renews the threat in identical terms precisely so that the fresh and surprising strategy of the ambush at the mulberry trees may stand out as a second, distinct act of divine guidance, and David's habit of inquiring afresh may be shown to meet a foe who has not learned his own lesson.
14:
A second inquiry meets a second, and different, answer, and the witnesses mark both the renewed dependence of the king and the strange precision of the divine tactics. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry notes that David enquired of God not once but again, upon this fresh occasion of the Philistines' invading him — the repetition itself a model, that in all our ways, and in each new difficulty, we should acknowledge God afresh rather than presume that yesterday's guidance will serve for today's battle. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott finds the first half of the verse here fuller and clearer than in the older account, while the second half, he says, must be adjusted by comparison with that earlier text, which reads: 'Thou shalt not go up; go round to their rear, and come upon them over against the baca trees.' The Chronicler's phrasing, he judges, has slightly transposed the terms, and the sense is best recovered from the parallel at 2 Samuel 5:23: not a frontal assault this time, but a wheeling movement to fall on the enemy from behind.

The 'mulberry trees' draw Ellicott's closest attention. The word behind them, he explains, occurs only here and in the parallel passage, and 'mulberry' is but the traditional Jewish rendering; more probably the tree meant is the balsam that the Arabs call baca, which sheds a gum like tears — whence its name, from the Hebrew root 'to weep,' the same word that gives the valley of weeping its name in Psalms 84:6. The grove of these weeping trees becomes the appointed place of ambush, the marker God sets for the manoeuvre.

The witnesses do not divide; together they bring out what is remarkable in the verse. The same God who at the first battle said plainly 'Go up' now says, as plainly, 'Go not up,' and prescribes instead a circuit to the rear and an approach by the balsam grove. David is not left to reason that what won the last field will win this one; he is given a wholly new plan, suited to a foe who has come by a new road. And because he asked again, he receives again — a fresh word for a fresh danger, so that his victory, when it comes, will be not the fruit of military routine but of obedience to a specific and unexpected command.
15:
The sign is a sound in the treetops, and here the witnesses openly divide over what that sound was. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports the Targum's frankly supernatural reading: 'When thou shalt hear the sound of the angels coming to thy assistance, then go out to battle; for an angel is sent from the presence of God, that he may render thy way prosperous.' On this understanding the rustling in the balsams is the tread of an unseen heavenly army marching ahead of David's own. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott inclines rather to a natural sign: David was to listen for the wind stirring in the tops of the baca trees, a sound like that of men walking over dead leaves, and at that signal to make his attack — though he grants a comparison with the mysterious sound of a great host that the LORD once made the Syrians hear in 2 Kings 7:6, which leaves the door ajar to something more than wind.

Ellicott sets the sign against a whole world of ancient belief, noting that all the old peoples attached a prophetic import to the motion and rustling of leaves — the tree-omens catalogued in the great Assyrian omen-work, the speaking oaks of Dodona, the diviners' oak of Judges 9:37. Yet these analogies, he insists, do not militate against the reality or the miraculous character of the biblical occurrence; the divine communications with man always take the form best fitted to strike the mind amid its reigning ideas, just as the visions of Scripture wear the colour of the seer's own world.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry passes over the mechanism to the obedience it demands: let the sound of the going in the tops of the mulberry trees direct us to attend God's motions, both in his providence and in the influences of his Spirit; when we perceive God to go before us, let us gird up our loins and follow him. Between Clarke's marching angels and Ellicott's rustling wind the difference is real — whether the noise was an audible miracle or a natural token divinely timed — but all three witnesses meet on the essential point. The sound, whatever its cause, was God's own signal that he had gone forth before his servant; David's part was neither to move too soon nor to hang back, but to wait for the tread in the treetops and then to strike, knowing the battle already the LORD's.
16:
David obeys to the letter, and the rout runs the length of a long day's pursuit. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott follows the wording as the victory unfolds: 'David did as God commanded him,' where the older text adds the little word 'so,' and 'they smote the host' — the enemy's camp — where that text says rather 'he smote the Philistines.' The chase, the verse says, ran 'from Gibeon even to Gazer,' and over that first name Ellicott pauses on a difference in the texts. The present Hebrew of the parallel reads 'Geba,' but the Septuagint there agrees with Chronicles in reading 'Gibeon,' while the Targum, Syriac, and Arabic support 'Geba'; Ellicott judges Gibeon the better reading, since it is favoured by the prophet's backward glance at 'the valley of Gibeon' in Isaiah 28:21. Gibeon lay some six miles north-west of Jerusalem, he notes, between the valley of Rephaim and Gezer, so that the line of flight makes good geographical sense.

The second name he treats more lightly: 'Gazer' is only the pausal form of Gezer, as Pharez stands for Perez; where Chronicles says 'even unto Gezer-ward,' the older text has 'until thou come to Gezer,' the border-town well known from Joshua 12:12. The enemy is driven clean out of the highlands and down toward the Philistine plain, their second invasion ending, like the first, in headlong ruin.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the encouragement he has been building toward. Let David's success, he says, embolden us to resist our spiritual enemies, in observance of divine directions and in dependence on divine strength: resist the devil, and he shall flee as the Philistines fled before David. The victory was won by exact obedience — David went round to the rear and waited for the sound, and only then struck — and the witnesses do not divide over its meaning. The same God who prescribed the tactic gave the triumph; the king who asked twice and obeyed precisely reaps a rout that sweeps from Gibeon to the gates of Gezer. What the chapter shows, on their common reading, is that guidance sought and guidance followed issue in a deliverance as complete as the danger was real, the whole Philistine power broken along the very road it had come to conquer by.
17:
The chapter ends not with a battle but with a reputation, and the witnesses read David's spreading fame as the Chronicler's own theological signature. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke keeps the horizon modest and exact: 'into all lands' means all the surrounding, neighbouring lands and nations, for no others can be intended. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes first that this closing verse is not in the older narrative at all; it has the look of a concluding reflection added by the Chronicler himself, of a piece with the summary notices he elsewhere appends to a reign, and the phrase 'the fame of David went out' recurs in just such a place at 2 Chronicles 26:15.

The theology of the verse is what Ellicott most wants seen. 'The LORD brought the fear of him upon all nations' — and yet, he grants, that fear was, as we should say, the natural effect of David's victories; the point is that in the Chronicler's view David's success in arms, with all its consequences, was itself the work of Jehovah. The dread that fell on the surrounding peoples was no mere accident of reputation but the doing of God, in language, Ellicott notes, much like that which describes the fear of the Jews falling on the nations in the days of Esther at Esther 8:17. The historian will not let even so worldly a thing as a king's renown be told without tracing it to the hand that raised him — the same confession with which the chapter began, that it was the LORD who had lifted David's kingdom on high.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the whole notice into type. Let the reputation David obtained, he says — not only in his own kingdom but among all his neighbours — be looked upon as a figure of the exalted honour of the Son of David. The fame of David went out into all lands, and he was admired by all people; and thus has God highly exalted our Redeemer and given him a name above every name, as Paul proclaims in Philippians 2:9. The witnesses do not divide. From Clarke's careful 'neighbouring nations' to Ellicott's 'the work of Jehovah' to Henry's greater Son, they close the chapter as it opened — on the confession that David's rise, his victories, and now his renown are all of God: a kingdom established, a house built, an enemy broken, and a name spread abroad, every clause of it the Lord's doing and marvellous in the reader's eyes.
